coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of orange fluorescent carbon dots and their application in fabrication of warm WLEDs and fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Varatharajan, Pandiaraja
Banu, I.B. Shameem
Mamat, Mohamad Hafiz
Vasimalai, Nagamalai
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we are reporting the synthesis of orange fluorescent carbon quantum dots (OFCDs) using o-phenylenediamine and semicarbazide by hydrothermal method and the synthesized OFCDs were applied for warm white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) and fluorescent ink applications. The fluorescence emission spectrum of OFCDs shows the emission maximum at 625 nm with an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. The quantum yield of OFCDs was calculated to be 23%. We have prepared warm WLED device by coating PVA-OFCDs polymer matrix on near UV LED light (λem: 420 nm). The OFCDs based warm WLED device exhibits chromaticity coordinate (0.42, 0.48), Colour Correlated Temperature (CCT) 2500 K and Color Rendering Index (CRI) value 82. We also prepared the highly stable fluorescent ink using the same PVA-OFCDs for security information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. This OFCDs and their WLEDs device would be an efficient one in optoelectronics and forensic fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange fluorescence
Light emitting diode
Anti-counterfeiting
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–visible and fluorescence spectral studies of OFCDs
XRD pattern and Raman spectral analysis
Thermogravimetric analysis of OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs
Security information encryption and anti-counterfeiting application

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the HR- TEM FACILITY at SRMIST set up with support from MNRE (Project No. 31/03/2014-15/PVSE-R&D ), Government of India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis spectrum of OFCDs, (B) Fluorescence spectrum of OFCDs with excitation wavelength of 540 nm. Inset: Photographs of OFCDs under (i) day light and (ii) near UV light, Fluorescence spectra of OFCDs with different excitation wavelengths (C) (400–540 nm) and (D) (540–580 nm).
FT-IR spectra of (a) semicarbazide (b) OPD and (c) OFCDs.
HR-TEM image of OFCDs. Inset: lattice fringes of OFCDs.
(A) XRD pattern of OFCDs and (B) Raman spectrum of OFCDs.
TGA curves of (A) OFCDs and (B) PVA-OFCDs.
X-ray Photoelectron spectra of OFCDs (A) survey spectrum, (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) O1s species.
CIE chromaticity diagram of OFCDs under the excitation wavelength 420 nm, inset: (i) uncoated near UV LED with 420 nm emission (ii) PVA-OFCDs based warm WLED.
Photographs of secret code encrypted filter papers (A and C) under day light and (B and D) under UV light.
Photographs of OFCDs coated currency notes (A) under day light and (B) under UV light.
Schematic representation for synthesizing of OFCDs.
Schematic representation of the preparation of warm WLEDs.
Comparison table of reported carbon dots, starting material, methodology, synthesis environment, fluorescence, size, quantum yield and applications with the present method.

row.entry: Long synthesis time, and hazard solvent used in the synthesis
High temperature condition and much higher particle size
Long synthesis time, high temperature condition and lower wavelength emission
Lower wavelength emission and low QY
High temperature and hazardous solvent used for synthesis
Lower QY, long synthesis time and high temperature
High temperature condition and lower wavelength emission
Warm WLED, information encryption and anti-counter feiting

Novelty: (i) New combination of precursors
(ii) Longer wavelength emission (Orange)
(iii) Excitation independent fluorescence behavior
(iv) High quantum yield, (v) Successfully developed warm white LED device and (vi) OFCDs were used for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting application

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of orange fluorescent carbon dots and their application in fabrication of warm WLEDs and fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science & Technology, Vandalur, Chennai, 600 048, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science & Technology, Vandalur, Chennai-600 048, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NANO-ElecTronic Centre (NET), School of Electrical Engineering, College of Engineering, Universiti Teknologi MARA, 40450, Shah Alam, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NANO-ElecTronic Centre (NET), School of Electrical Engineering, College of Engineering, Universiti Teknologi MARA, 40450 Shah Alam, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science & Technology, Vandalur, Chennai, 600 048, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science & Technology, Vandalur, Chennai-600 048, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we are reporting the synthesis of orange fluorescent carbon quantum dots (OFCDs) using o-phenylenediamine and semicarbazide by hydrothermal method and the synthesized OFCDs were applied for warm white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) and fluorescent ink applications. The fluorescence emission spectrum of OFCDs shows the emission maximum at 625 nm with an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. The quantum yield of OFCDs was calculated to be 23%. We have prepared warm WLED device by coating PVA-OFCDs polymer matrix on near UV LED light (λem: 420 nm). The OFCDs based warm WLED device exhibits chromaticity coordinate (0.42, 0.48), Colour Correlated Temperature (CCT) 2500 K and Color Rendering Index (CRI) value 82. We also prepared the highly stable fluorescent ink using the same PVA-OFCDs for security information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. This OFCDs and their WLEDs device would be an efficient one in optoelectronics and forensic fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm. The CDs are widely used in a variety of fields, including biosensor, catalysis, cancer therapy, bio-imaging, drug delivery and light emitting diodes (LEDs), due to their high aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, high fluorescence and low photo-bleaching [1–3]. The origin of the CDs fluorescence is highly depends on the surface defects and quantum size confinement effect [4–6].

Numerous researches have been published recently about heteroatoms doped carbon dots, which emit fluorescence at various wavelengths [7]. Doped carbon dots with nitrogen, phosphorus, sulphur, and boron are an alternate material for a variety of uses. Particularly, optoelectronics, bio-imaging, biosensing, optoelectronics, energy and environmental applications [8,9]. Nitrogen can easily connect with carbon dots due to its comparable atomic structure and high electronegativity [10,11]. Additionally, the fluorescence can be shifted to longer wavelength regions via π conjugated system formed by nitrogen-related energy states, which is present in the carbon dots. It could therefore be effectively applied to warm white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) applications [12].

Compared to incandescent and compact fluorescent lights (CFL), solid state LEDs are a far more prominent source of light. Commercial WLEDs are often made with blue LEDs and rare earth doped yellow emitting phosphors, and these WLEDs have Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT) values exceeding 6000 K, making them suitable for outdoor illumination [13]. To avoid eye fatigue, white light with a reddish hue and a CCT value closer to 3000 K is strongly preferred for indoor lighting. In particularly, warm WLEDs which were prepared by nitrogen doped carbon dots are showing CCT value less than 3000 K [14].

The solid state emission of carbon dots are a key component for WLEDs applications. Carbon dots can undergo the self-quenching in solid state, due to Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). There are several ways to prevent the self-quenching of carbon dots [15]. A particularly effective method for WLED application is incorporating carbon dots with the polymer matrix. Generally, polyethylene glycol (PEG), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) could act as a polymer matrix to prevent fluorescence quenching of carbon dots in solid state [16–18].

In addition to WLED applications, carbon dots can be used for applications involving data security and invisible labelling. In the modern era, data security and invisible labeling are inevitable necessity in many industries, government sectors and company brands to overcome the counterfeiting problems. For the time being, a variety of security encryption techniques, including laser holograms, RFID, and rapid response codes (QR codes) are being used to safeguard and transmit sensitive data. In this particular sector, optical technologies have had an advantage over the other methods because of their quick response times, high levels of stability, and user-friendly design. Many fluorescent labelling products are constructed of semiconductor quantum dots, lanthanide-based fluorescent materials, and organic fluorescent dyes. However, it has some drawbacks such as heavy metal emission and environmental toxicity that restrict their usage in various industries. Most recently, carbon dots based fluorescent inks are often used for security code encryption, secrete code exchange and anti-counterfeiting applications, because of their less toxicity, easy processing, high fluorescence emission, high stability, biocompatibility, low cost and low photo-bleaching effect [19–21]. Hence, it is an essential to prepare the highly fluorescent carbon dots for WLEDs and anti-counterfeiting applications.

Herein, we report the synthesis of orange fluorescent carbon dots using nitrogen rich semicarbazide and o-phenylenediamine (OFCDs) by hydrothermal method. Then we have applied the synthesized OFCDs for the preparation of warm WLEDs device. Further, we have utilized the OFCDs for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications.

o-Phnylenediamine, semicarbazide, polyvinyl alcohol, ethanol, sodium chloride, sulphuric acid, hydrochloric acid, quinine sulfate, sodium hydroxide, disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate. All these analytical grade chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were used to prepare pH buffers (from pH 2 to pH 11) and pH of buffer solution adjusted by HCl and NaOH. Whatman No.1 grade filter paper with 110 mm diameter purchased from amazon, 420 nm Near Ultra Violet LED (n-UV LED, 3W) was brought from the Mercy electronics, Chennai-48. Kids bank currency notes and Reynolds ball point pen were purchased from nearby stationary shop.

We have synthesized OFCDs by hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, 0.1g of semicarbazide and 40 mg of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of double distilled water and then the mixture was transferred into Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave which was kept in the muffle furnace at 180 °C for 12 h with the temperature rise of 4 °C/min. After this step, autoclave allowed cooling to room temperature. Then, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the centrifuged solution was filtered by
0.22
μ m
syringe filter. The resultant solution was stored in a refrigerator.

Poly-vinyl alcohol (1g) was well dissolved in 10 ml of hot water. Then, OFCDs were mixed with PVA solution (1:1 vol ratio) and stirred the solution for 8 h. The obtained PVA incorporated OFCDs were used for the preparation of warm WLEDs.

The prepared PVA-OFCDs were used as the material to fabricate warm WLED. The PVA-OFCDs solution were coated on a glass mold and dried at room temperature. After drying PVA-OFCDs film was peeled off from the glass mold (Scheme 2 ). The prepared PVA-OFCDs film was placed on UV LED light (λem: 420 nm) to observe the emission process. A bright warm white light emission was observed on the film.

The PVA mixed OFCDs were used directly as fluorescent ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. The fluorescent ink was filled in an empty pen and used as fluorescent ink pen. Then the pen was used for making fluorescent secret codes encryption and anti-counterfeiting symbols.

X-ray diffraction study was performed by BRUKER XRD with wavelength λ = 1.5406 Å (Ni-Filtered Cu Kα radiation) and it was performed in the scan range 2θ: 10°–90° with scanning rate: 6°/min. Raman spectral analysis was performed by Confocal Raman spectroscopy Alpha 300R (Germany) in the scan range of 1200 cm−1 to 1800 cm−1. This technique is used for the confirmation of the graphitic structure of carbon dots. The Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (JASCO 6300) was used to measure the FT-IR spectra of carbon dots and FT-IR spectra were measured in the scan range of 400 cm−1 to 4400 cm−1. It is used to illustrate the chemical moieties present in the carbon dots. The High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEOL-2100-japan-operated condition at 200 kV) was used to take nanoscale images of our carbon nanomaterial. A Shimidzu TA60 model was used to conduct a thermal gravimetric investigation. Identification of C, N, and O chemical bonds were analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) studies which were recorded by ULVAC-PHI PHI 5000 VersaProb instrument at the binding energy range of 0–800 eV. The absorption spectra were measured by PerkinElmer Lambda25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer in the spectral range of 200 nm–800 nm. Fluorescence emission spectra and relative quantum yield were measured by PerkinElmer lambda LS45 fluorescence spectrophotometer. All the fluorescence measurements were performed in the spectral range of 550–800 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible and fluorescence spectral studies of OFCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–Vis spectrum of OFCDs is shown in Fig. 1 A. The absorption peak obtained at 286 nm corresponds to
π − π *
electron transition of CC bonds. The broad band observed at 430 nm is the reference peak of amine groups [22,23]. The UV–Vis characteristic peaks of OPD (280 nm) and semicarbazide (210 nm) does not appear in the UV–Vis spectrum of OFCDs (Fig. S1). This confirms the formation of OFCDs.

Fig. 1B shows the emission spectrum of OFCDs. The OFCDs exhibit an emission maximum in 625 nm at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. We have studied the emission behaviour of OFCDs with different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1 (C and D)). For all the excitation wavelengths, OFCDs give emission at 625 nm. This phenomenon is called as excitation-independent fluorescence behaviour. When the excitation wavelength changes from 400 to 540 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases and attains maximum intensity at the 540 nm excitation wavelength without any shift in the emission wavelength. (Fig. 1C). Beyond the 540 nm of excitation wavelength (from 550 to 580 nm), the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases without any shift in the emission wavelength (λem = 625 nm) (Fig. 1D). Hence we fixed 540 nm as an excitation wavelength for further studies. The fluorescence behaviour of freshly prepared and 3 months old carbon dots remains unchanged. Thus, the OFCDs remained stable for 3 months (Fig. S2).

The origin of the fluorescence in the carbon dots is due to the surface defects. In nitrogen doped carbon dots, fluorescence is originated from the different nitrogen related compounds and conjugated aromatic
π
-system [12]. According to the literature reports, the conjugated aromatic
π
-system led to a red shift of the emission wavelength of carbon dots. The presence of nitrogen related compounds on the surface of the OFCDs, helps to form the conjugated aromatic
π
-system, numerous nitrogen energy states, and also decreases the band gap. Because of the surface defects and nitrogen related functional groups present in the OFCDs, give longer wavelength emission and the excitation-independent fluorescence behaviour [12,24–26].

For warm WLED application, OFCDs were functionalized with PVA to maintain the fluorescence at a solid state. When PVA is mixed with OFCDs, functional groups present in the OFCDs interact with the hydroxyl groups of PVA. Which increases the hydrogen bonding between PVA and OFCDs [27,28].

The fluorescence properties of PVA-OFCDs were studied. The results show PVA-OFCDs exhibit excitation-independent fluorescence behaviour with same excitation and emission wavelength like OFCDs (Fig. S3). The quantum yield value of PVA-OFCDs is 26%, which is higher than OFCDs. Increased quantum yield is brought on by the functionalization of PVA on the surface of OFCDs, which stabilizes the surface traps and reduces non-radiative recombination [29,30].

We have characterized the synthesized OFCDs and their starting materials by FT-IR spectral technique. The FT-IR spectra of (a) semicarbazide, (b) OPD, and (c) OFCDs are shown in Fig. 2 . FT-IR spectra of semicarbazide (Fig. 2; curve a) and OPD (Fig. 2; curve b) have shown the characteristic stretching vibration peaks of N–H, O–H and C–H appeared at 3500 cm−1 to 2500 cm−1. N–H, C–N and N–CO stretching and bending vibration peaks are exhibited from 2000 cm−1 to 512 cm−1 [31]. The FT-IR spectra of OPD have illustrated C–C, C–N and C–O stretching vibration peaks from 1600 cm−1 to 1152 cm−1 [32]. This confirmed the presence of N–H, COOH and CH2 groups in structure of the semicarbazide and OPD.

Whereas, the FT-IR spectrum of OFCDs exhibits a broad peak from 3500 to 2750 cm−1 that reflects the N–H, O–H, and C–H stretching vibration peaks [33]. The observed peaks at 1707 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 are assigned to be CO and CC stretching vibration peaks, respectively. The CC stretching vibration peak is reflect the formation of carbon-carbon bond in the core of carbon dots. The N–H stretching and bending vibration peaks are shown by the peaks at 1369 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1 [34–36]. The benzene derivatives are shown by the low intensity peaks above 700 cm−1. From above results, we proved the successful formation of OFCDs and which contain carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups.

HR-TEM image of OFCDs is shown in Fig. 3 . HR-TEM image shows highly mono-dispersive spherical carbon dots particles. The average particles size was calculated using Image J software [37]. The particle size was calculated to be 4.71 ± 1.24 nm. Fig. 4 , the inset shows the HR-TEM image of OFCDs in 2 nm magnification, which illustrates the atomic lattice fringes and graphitic nature of the carbon dots. The lattice spacing was calculated to be 0.25 nm and it is corresponds to the [100] facet of graphene [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD pattern and Raman spectral analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of OFCDs is shown in Fig. 4A. The broad peak at 27.1° corresponds to the graphene plane [002], which typically indicates the interlayer graphitic structure [39]. Fig. 4B shows the Raman spectrum of OFCDs. It consists of two peaks, which are known as D-band (1362 cm−1) and G-band (1781 cm−1). The formation of the D-band is due to the sp3-hybridization of the carbon network which represents the amount of defects and functionalization of OFCDs. The G-band (sp2-hybridization) indicates the graphitization associated with the OFCDs. The intensity ratio between D-band and G-band has been found to be 1.1. This higher ID/IG ratio speculate that OFCDs has a large amount of surface defects [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analysis of OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve of OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs are shown in Fig. 5 A and B, which illustrates the relation between degradation temperature and weight loss percentage. The synthesized OFCDs were degraded by 3 step processes. 1st step degradation occurred at 180–238 °C with a weight loss of 14.02%, which is due to degradation of hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and amine (-NH2) groups present in the OFCDs [41]. The sharp 2nd step degradation was occurred between 247 and 326 °C, which are attributed to the pyrolysis of the core structure of OFCDs [42]. The total weight loss of the 2nd step degradation was calculated to be 57.32%. The degradation of pyridine related compounds were occurred between 421 and 600 °C with the total weight loss 7.85%. The degradation curve becomes flat about 600 °C, and no more degradation was shown [43].

We have incorporated the OFCDs with PVA to stop the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots in the solid state [18]. The TGA curve of PVA incorporated OFCDs (PVA-OFCDs) is shown in Fig. 5B. The degradation curve of PVA-OFCDs is similar to OFCDs. However, an extended step of degradation was found between 169 and 389 °C, which is consistent with PVA polymer matrix degradation [44].

The functional groups of the OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs are started to degrade at 180 °C and 200 °C, respectively. From this results, we can conclude that the OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs are stable up to 180 °C and 200 °C, respectively [45,46]. However, due to the quenching of the fluorescence in the solid state, OFCDs cannot be used directly for WLED applications. We have incorporated PVA into OFCDs to prevent the fluorescence quenching of OFCDs in solid state. The TGA results were revealed that the PVA incorporated OFCDs are stable up to 200 °C. Hence, we used PVA-OFCDs to fabricate warm WLEDs [47].

The chemicals species present in the OFCDs were analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectral technique (XPS). Fig. 6 A, shows the survey spectrum of OFCDs which indicate three distinct peaks at 286 eV, 402 eV and 534 eV which refer to the C1s, N1s and O1s spectrum respectively.

The peak at 286 eV (C1s) is deconvoluted into three peaks at 286.64 eV, 289.64 eV and 285.16 eV, which belongs to C–O, O–CO and C–N bond, respectively. This confirms that nitrogen and oxygen compounds are present on the surface of OFCDs (Fig. 6B) [48,49]. Two deconvoluted peaks were seen in N1s spectrum at 402.95 eV and 400.65 eV. These peaks corresponds to nitrogen oxide (ON–C) and pyridinic N–H compounds, respectively (Fig. 6C) [50–52]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s shows two deconvoluted peaks at 533 eV and 534.9 eV, which is attributed to carbonyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of OFCDs- (Fig. 6D) [53,54]. Thus, XPS spectra revealed the presence of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of OFCDs.

The ionic strength study was performed with different sodium chloride concentration. Fig. S4, shows fluorescence intensity of OFCDs vs ionic strength concentration of NaCl. Fluorescence intensity of OFCDs is found to vary at different NaCl concentrations. When the NaCl concentration was increased from 50 mM to 150 mM, the fluorescence intensity of OFCDs was stable. Beyond 150 mM, the fluorescence intensity of OFCDs decreases gradually. Thus, we found that OFCDs have high ionic strength and tolerance level up to 150 mM.

We studied the pH effects on OFCDs by analysing the fluorescence intensity changes for various pH values (pH 2 to pH 11) (Fig. S5). Li et al. reported, the pH dependency of carbon dots mainly depends on the protonation and de-protonation of oxygen contained groups present in the carbon dots surface. Due to the protonation and deprotonation processes, the molecular structure or electronic structure of carbon dots was changed at different pH, that leads to changes in the fluorescent intensity of carbon dots. It is the most accepted mechanism for pH dependency in carbon dots [55–58]. As reported pH dependency mechanism, the fluorescence intensity of OFCDs is high at acidic medium (pH 2 to pH 5) whereas at pH 6 and 7, there is small decrease in the fluorescence intensity. In the alkaline pH 8 to pH 11, the fluorescence intensity gets quenched.

William's comparative method was used for the calculation of the Quantum Yield (QY) of OFCDs. By comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity of unknown sample and the reference standard, the quantum yield of an unknown material is determined. Rhodamine B was used as the reference standard material for QY measurement (QY = 31%). The following equation was used to calculate the quantum yield of unknown substance:
(1)
Q Y
X
=
F x
*
A s
*
Q Y
s
F s
*
A x
where, “As” and “Ax” refers the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the standard (Rhodamine B) and OFCDs respectively. “Fx” denotes the integrated fluorescence intensity of OFCDs and integrated fluorescence intensity of the standard (Rhodamine B) is denoted as “Fs”. “QYx” and “QYs” are the quantum yield of OFCDs and the reference standard (Rhodamine B QY = 0.31) respectively [59,60]. We measured quantum yield of OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs by the above method and the calculated quantum yields are 23% and 26%, respectively.

The OFCDs were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymer matrix to enhance the fluorescence of OFCDs in solid state. The detailed procedure for the fabrication of warm WLEDs using OFCDs is given in the experimental section 2.4. We coated PVA mixed OFCDs on the n-UV LED light (λem: 420 nm) and allowed to dry at room temperature. Thus PVA-OFCDs fabricated on n-UV LED was prepared. When PVA-OFCDs fabricated UV LED was turned on, the warm white light was emitted.

Fig. S6 shows the fluorescence spectrum of PVA-OFCDs (λex: 420 nm). The observed the emission maximum is at 625 nm. The prepared PVA-OFCDs have shown good photo stability even after irradiation of UV light for 150 min (Fig. S7). Fig. 6 illustrates the CIE diagram of PVA-OFCDs. The CIE chromaticity coordinate was found to be (0.42, 0.48) in the CIE diagram for PVA-OFCDs under 420 nm excitation wavelength. This chromaticity coordinate matches with that of reports on warm white emission coordinate [61]. The Colour Correlated Temperature (CCT) and Colour Rendering Index (CRI) value was obtained to be 2500 K and 82 respectively (Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7 inset (i,ii) shows the photographs of n-UV LED and warm WLED respectively [62,63]. We also recorded the chromaticity with an excitation wavelength at 540 nm. Interestingly, the chromaticity coordinates (0.64, 0.35) obtained, which located in the deep orange colour region in the CIE diagram (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Security information encryption and anti-counterfeiting application
ce:section.ce:para: The longer wavelength emissive carbon dots are highly advantageous material for fluorescent ink based applications due to quick identification, high fluorescence and low photo bleaching properties. The preparation procedure of fluorescent ink is given in the experimental details (Section 2.5.). The PVA mixed OFCDs were used as fluorescent ink for the information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. The fluorescent ink filled pen (FIP) was used for writing daylight invisible information and symbols on the Whatman No.1 filter paper and currency notes. For encrypting secret codes, secret PIN numbers (4713) was written using FIP on Whatman No.1 filter paper. When examined in visible light, fluorescent ink coated numbers were not visible under daylight. The secret PIN ″4713″ was clearly visible when examined under UV light, and the uncoated area had no colour. The images of fluorescent ink based Whatman No.1 filter papers under daylight and UV light, respectively, are shown in Fig. 8 A and B.

In this work, FIP was used to encrypt the ASCII code in addition to the applications of OFCDs mentioned above in order to demonstrate their versatility. ASCII codes are mainly used for information exchange purposes. These codes are made up of “0″ and “1″ shaped alphabetic characters. We randomly wrote various alphabetical ASCII codes with some secret information (114,110,117) on Whatman No. 1 filter paper for this purpose. The ASCII codes 114,110,117 were selected to examine the fluorescent ink and were overwritten by FIP. These ASCII codes were then examined under UV light and the coated secrete ASCII codes were observed to glow under UV light. While in daylight, these codes are visible like other normal numbers. Thus the ASCII codes were identified using the fluorescent ink prepared via PVA-OFCDs. Fig. 8C and D presents the photographs of the ASCII codes encrypted filter papers when examined under day light and UV light respectively. The above discussion of our work emphasis that, using the prepared fluorescent ink the secret codes can be exchanged very efficiently and fluorescent ink has also proven effective in security-related applications.

The application of the OFCDs in the anti-counterfeiting was tested using the prepared fluorescent ink by coating it on the currency notes (Fig. 9 A and B). A small portion of the currency was coated with fluorescent ink and allowed to dry for 3 min at room temperature. When examined with visible and UV light, it was observed that while the coated part of the currency was not visible under day light but it seen in UV light. An anti-counterfeiting strategy based on optical method is an essential today. Since fluorescent ink made with OFCDs is a cheap, environmentally benign substance with strong chemical stability, it is excellent for forensic applications.

When we compared the carbon dots, starting material, methodology, synthesis environment, fluorescence, size, quantum yield and applications with the present method, our carbon dots have shown good results. Comparison of present work with other reported work given in Table 1 .

The nanostructured orange fluorescent carbon dots (OFCDs) were synthesized successfully by hydrothermal method using semicarbazide and o-phenylenediamine as the starting material. The synthesized OFCDs were stable for more than 3 months. The relative quantum yields of OFCDs and PVA-OFCDs were calculated to be 23% and 26%, respectively. The warm WLED device was fabricated using the PVA-OFCDs, it shows high Colour Rendering Index (CRI), low Colour Correlated Temperature (CCT) 2500 K and suitable chromaticity coordinate (0.42, 0.48) which are essential for warm WLED application. The fluorescent ink obtained from the PVA-OFCDs demonstrated that, it is perfect material for the information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. Because, the FIP written numbers and drawn symbols on Whatman No1 filter papers and bank currency notes are clearly visible in UV light. Thus, we have concluded through the fabricated warm WLED performance, data-encryption and anti-counterfeiting performance that the novel OFCDs synthesized in this work could be a promising material for the demanding applications of optoelectronics and forensic sciences.

Mr. P. Varatharajan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization- original draft. Dr. I. B. Shameem Banu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Dr. M. H. B. Mamat: Project administration, Supervision. Dr. N. Vasimalai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This article is dedicated to the memory of Professor Dr. S. Abraham John, Director, Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed to be University, Gandhigram-624 302, Tamilnadu, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physb.2023.414703.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: a review
Anal. Methods
